categories.name: IT'S ALL FROM GREEK TO ME

questions.clue: The Greek word zelos gave us this adjective meaning "envious"
This word for a type of gymnast with no fear of heights comes in part from a Greek word for "highest"
The name of this device is from Greek for "spiral" & "wing", not that the ancient Greeks ever saw one
Now often meaning a part of a song, in ancient Greece it meant a band of dramatic singers & dancers
From the Greek for "long-lasting", this term for a good buddy comes from 17th century Cambridge University slang
This highest peak in the lower 48 is at the eastern border of Sequoia National Park
Katharine Lee Bates wrote "America The Beautiful" after visiting this mountain & has a statue in Colorado Springs
Just north of the Alps, these mountains in France & Switzerland may get their name from a word meaning "forest"
To complete the Seven Summits, you have to top this Antarctic mountain
The highest point in the Atlas Mountains is this peak that's south of Marrakech
A motto of this now-sainted nun was "Let us do something beautiful for God"
When fashion designer Paul Poiret asked her, "For whom are you in mourning?", she replied, "For you"
Milliner Lilly Daché  designed tutti-frutti headgear for this Portuguese-born Brazilian movie star
In 1935 she became director of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Training Institute

This Brit worked tirelessly for women's suffrage & died just three weeks before the UK Equal Franchise Act was adopted in 1928
This economic -ism that uses tariffs to bolster domestic industry helped get William McKinley elected in 1896
This agency that collects tariffs at U.S. ports of entry was formed in 2003 as part of the Dept. of Homeland Security
Aimed at boosting economic recovery after WWII, the General Agreement on Tariffs & Trade led to the creation of this group in 1995
Tariffs were a major source of government revenue until 1913, when passage of this constitutional amendment led to a federal income tax
senate.gov called this 1930 piece of tariff legislation "among the most catastrophic acts in congressional history"
